subject: Physics
text: Charge- Charge is the property associated with matter due to which it produces and experiences electric and magnetic effect.SI unit of charge is coulomb.
text_html: Charge- Charge is the property associated with matter due to which it produces and experiences electric and magnetic effect.SI unit of charge is coulomb.

subject: Physics
text: Amber and Electricity Thales of Miletus discovered that amber rubbed with wool or silk attracts light objects. The word electricity derives from the Greek word elektron , meaning amber.
text_html:
Amber and Electricity
  • Thales of Miletus discovered that amber rubbed with wool or silk attracts light objects.
  • The word electricity derives from the Greek word elektron, meaning amber.

subject: Physics
text: Electrification and Attraction/Repulsion Materials like glass rods rubbed with silk repel similar charged rods but attract opposite charges like silk or wool. Plastic rods rubbed with fur show similar behaviors — they repel each other but attract the fur.
text_html:
Electrification and Attraction/Repulsion
  • Materials like glass rods rubbed with silk repel similar charged rods but attract opposite charges like silk or wool.
  • Plastic rods rubbed with fur show similar behaviors — they repel each other but attract the fur.

subject: Physics
text: Charge Classification From experiments, it was determined there are two types of electric charges. Like charges repel; unlike charges attract. Polarity of charge is a property distinguishing the two charges.
text_html:
Charge Classification
  • From experiments, it was determined there are two types of electric charges.
  • Like charges repel; unlike charges attract.
  • Polarity of charge is a property distinguishing the two charges.

subject: Physics
text: Charge Neutralization When a charged body touches another, it loses its charge. This neutralization implies the charges cancel each other out. Charges are termed positive and negative .
text_html:
Charge Neutralization
  • When a charged body touches another, it loses its charge.
  • This neutralization implies the charges cancel each other out.
  • Charges are termed positive and negative.

subject: Physics
text: Definition of Charges By convention, charge on glass rod or cat's fur is called positive. Charge on plastic rod or silk is termed negative. An object with no charge is electrically neutral.
text_html:
Definition of Charges
  • By convention, charge on glass rod or cat's fur is called positive.
  • Charge on plastic rod or silk is termed negative.
  • An object with no charge is electrically neutral.

subject: Physics
text: Gold-leaf Electroscope A device used to detect charge is the gold-leaf electroscope. It consists of a metal rod with two thin gold leaves. The degree of leaf divergence shows the charge amount.
text_html:
Gold-leaf Electroscope
  • A device used to detect charge is the gold-leaf electroscope.
  • It consists of a metal rod with two thin gold leaves.
  • The degree of leaf divergence shows the charge amount.

subject: Physics
text: Understanding Charge in Material All matter is comprised of atoms and molecules. Although neutral, materials contain charges that balance each other. Forces within materials are electrical, arising from charged particles.
text_html:
Understanding Charge in Material
  • All matter is comprised of atoms and molecules.
  • Although neutral, materials contain charges that balance each other.
  • Forces within materials are electrical, arising from charged particles.

subject: Physics
text: Charging a Neutral Body To charge, we add or remove a particular charge. Excess or deficit of charge indicates a charged state. Electrons in solids transfer between bodies when charging occurs.
text_html:
Charging a Neutral Body
  • To charge, we add or remove a particular charge.
  • Excess or deficit of charge indicates a charged state.
  • Electrons in solids transfer between bodies when charging occurs.

subject: Physics
text: Mechanism of Charging A body is positively charged by losing electrons. Similarly, gaining electrons results in a negative charge. Example: Rubbing glass rod with silk moves electrons to silk.
text_html:
Mechanism of Charging
  • A body is positively charged by losing electrons.
  • Similarly, gaining electrons results in a negative charge.
  • Example: Rubbing glass rod with silk moves electrons to silk.

subject: Physics
text: Laws of Conservation No new charge is created during the rubbing process. Transferred electrons are a tiny fraction of the total electrons. This understanding helps explain static electricity phenomena.
text_html:
Laws of Conservation
  • No new charge is created during the rubbing process.
  • Transferred electrons are a tiny fraction of the total electrons.
  • This understanding helps explain static electricity phenomena.

subject: Physics
text: Conductors Substances that allow the passage of electricity easily are known as conductors. Metals, human and animal bodies, and earth are examples.
text_html:
Conductors
Substances that allow the passage of electricity easily are known as conductors. Metals, human and animal bodies, and earth are examples.

subject: Physics
text: Conductivity in Conductors Electrons in conductors are relatively free to move, allowing electricity to pass through with ease.
text_html:
Conductivity in Conductors
Electrons in conductors are relatively free to move, allowing electricity to pass through with ease.

subject: Physics
text: Insulators Materials that resist the passage of electricity, such as glass, porcelain, plastic, nylon, and wood, are called insulators.
text_html:
Insulators
Materials that resist the passage of electricity, such as glass, porcelain, plastic, nylon, and wood, are called insulators.

subject: Physics
text: Distribution of Charge In conductors, transferred charge spreads over the whole surface, whereas in insulators, charges stay localized.
text_html:
Distribution of Charge
In conductors, transferred charge spreads over the whole surface, whereas in insulators, charges stay localized.

subject: Physics
text: Plastic Comb vs. Metal Spoon A plastic or nylon comb can get electrified on rubbing, unlike a metal spoon, because the charge in metals dissipates due to body conductivity.
text_html:
Plastic Comb vs. Metal Spoon
A plastic or nylon comb can get electrified on rubbing, unlike a metal spoon, because the charge in metals dissipates due to body conductivity.

subject: Physics
text: Charging of Metal with Non-conductive Handle A metal rod with a plastic handle shows charging signs when rubbed, as the handle prevents contact with conductive surfaces.
text_html:
Charging of Metal with Non-conductive Handle
A metal rod with a plastic handle shows charging signs when rubbed, as the handle prevents contact with conductive surfaces.

subject: Physics
text: Semiconductors There exists a third category called semiconductors that have resistance levels between those of conductors and insulators.
text_html:
Semiconductors
There exists a third category called semiconductors that have resistance levels between those of conductors and insulators.

subject: Physics
text: Gold Leaf Electroscope A tool used to detect electric charges through the divergence of gold leaves, indicating the presence of electric charge.
text_html:
Gold Leaf Electroscope
A tool used to detect electric charges through the divergence of gold leaves, indicating the presence of electric charge.

subject: Physics
text: Basic Electroscope Design A simple electroscope can be designed to detect electric charge and consists of a conductive element connected to thin leaves.
text_html:
Basic Electroscope Design
  • A simple electroscope can be designed to detect electric charge and consists of a conductive element connected to thin leaves.

subject: Physics
text: Charge Leakage When a charged metal object touches a conductor, any excess charge will transfer to the object, often discharging to the ground.
text_html:
Charge Leakage
When a charged metal object touches a conductor, any excess charge will transfer to the object, often discharging to the ground.

subject: Physics
text: Properties of charges: Like charges repel and unlike charges attract. Charges are additive in nature i.e., Q = Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + ... + Qn Charges are quantized. i.e., Q = {tex}\pm{/tex} ne where n = 1, 2, 3, ...& e = 1.602 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -19 C. When any physical quantity exists in discrete packets rather than in continuous amount, the quantity is said to be quantised. Hence, charge is quantised. Charge in a body is independent of its velocity. Charge is conserved. To measure charge gold leaf electroscopes are used.
text_html:
Properties of charges:
Like charges repel and unlike charges attract.
Charges are additive in nature i.e., Q = Q1 + Q2 + Q3 + ... + Qn
Charges are quantized. i.e., Q = {tex}\pm{/tex} ne where n = 1, 2, 3, ...& e = 1.602 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C.
When any physical quantity exists in discrete packets rather than in continuous amount, the quantity is said to be quantised. Hence, charge is quantised.
Charge in a body is independent of its velocity.
Charge is conserved.
To measure charge gold leaf electroscopes are used.

subject: Physics
text: Types of Charges Electric charges are categorized into positive and negative charges. These two types tend to cancel out each other's effects.
text_html:
Types of Charges
Electric charges are categorized into positive and negative charges. These two types tend to cancel out each other's effects.

subject: Physics
text: Point Charges When charged bodies are small compared to the distances between them, they are considered as point charges, meaning that all the charge is assumed to be concentrated at a single point.
text_html:
Point Charges
When charged bodies are small compared to the distances between them, they are considered as point charges, meaning that all the charge is assumed to be concentrated at a single point.

subject: Physics
text: Additivity of Charges The total charge of a system is the sum of all individual charges: {tex} q_1 + q_2 + q_3 + ... + q_{{n}} {/tex} . Charges do not have direction, similar to mass, but unlike mass, charges can be negative.
text_html:
Additivity of Charges

  • The total charge of a system is the sum of all individual charges: {tex} q_1 + q_2 + q_3 + ... + q_{{n}} {/tex} .
  • Charges do not have direction, similar to mass, but unlike mass, charges can be negative.

subject: Physics
text: Charge Conservation The total charge in an isolated system is always conserved even though charges may get redistributed due to interactions.
text_html:
Charge Conservation
The total charge in an isolated system is always conserved even though charges may get redistributed due to interactions.

subject: Physics
text: Charge Creation and Destruction New charges cannot be created or destroyed. Processes may lead to the creation of charged particles, such as a neutron turning into a proton and an electron, maintaining the zero net charge.
text_html:
Charge Creation and Destruction
New charges cannot be created or destroyed. Processes may lead to the creation of charged particles, such as a neutron turning into a proton and an electron, maintaining the zero net charge.

subject: Physics
text: Quantisation of Charge Charges are integral multiples of a basic unit, denoted by {tex} e {/tex} , where {tex} q = n \times e {/tex} and {tex} n {/tex} is an integer. An electron has a charge of {tex} -e {/tex} , while a proton has a charge of {tex} +e {/tex} .
text_html:
Quantisation of Charge

  • Charges are integral multiples of a basic unit, denoted by {tex} e {/tex}, where {tex} q = n \times e {/tex} and {tex} n {/tex} is an integer.
  • An electron has a charge of {tex} -e {/tex}, while a proton has a charge of {tex} +e {/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Unit of Charge In the International System (SI), the unit of charge is the coulomb, denoted by C. 1 coulomb is the charge that flows through a wire in 1 second if the current is 1 ampere.
text_html:
Unit of Charge

  • In the International System (SI), the unit of charge is the coulomb, denoted by C.
  • 1 coulomb is the charge that flows through a wire in 1 second if the current is 1 ampere.

subject: Physics
text: Role of Electrons and Protons If only electrons and protons exist, all observable charges are multiples of {tex} e {/tex} . The total charge on a body is determined by the difference between the number of protons and electrons.
text_html:
Role of Electrons and Protons
If only electrons and protons exist, all observable charges are multiples of {tex} e {/tex}. The total charge on a body is determined by the difference between the number of protons and electrons.

subject: Physics
text: Charge Granularity At a macroscopic level, charge appears continuous due to the small magnitude of {tex} e {/tex} compared to total charge. However, at the microscopic level, charges appear in discrete quantities due to the quantisation.
text_html:
Charge Granularity
At a macroscopic level, charge appears continuous due to the small magnitude of {tex} e {/tex} compared to total charge. However, at the microscopic level, charges appear in discrete quantities due to the quantisation.

subject: Physics
text: Comparison with Geometrical Concepts The grainy nature of charge is likened to a dotted line that appears continuous from a distance, similarly at large scales, charge appears continuous.
text_html:
Comparison with Geometrical Concepts
The grainy nature of charge is likened to a dotted line that appears continuous from a distance, similarly at large scales, charge appears continuous.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: One coulomb: It is that charge which when placed at a distance of 1 m from an equal charge and similar charge in vacuum would repel it by a force of 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 9 N.
text_html: One coulomb: It is that charge which when placed at a distance of 1 m from an equal charge and similar charge in vacuum would repel it by a force of 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 109 N.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Coulomb's law: It states that the electrostatic force of interaction or repulsion acting between two stationary point charges is given by: {tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}{/tex} where q 1 and q 2 are the stationary point charges and r is the separation between them in air or vacuum. Also, {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9{/tex} N-m 2 /C 2 where, {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} = permittivity of free space = 8.85419 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 C 2 /N-m 2 Now, {tex}\frac{F_{\text {vacuum }}}{F}=\frac{\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}, \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}}{\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon}, \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}=\varepsilon_r{/tex} where, {tex}\varepsilon_r{/tex} is called relative permittivity of the medium also called the dielectric constant of the medium. In vector form, {tex}\mathbf{\vec F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{|\mathbf{\vec r}|^2} \hat{\mathbf{r}}{/tex}
text_html:
Coulomb's law: It states that the electrostatic force of interaction or repulsion acting between two stationary point charges is given by:
{tex}F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}{/tex}
where q1 and q2 are the stationary point charges and r is the separation between them in air or vacuum.
Also, {tex}\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9{/tex} N-m2/C2
where, {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} = permittivity of free space = 8.85419 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12 C2/N-m2
Now, {tex}\frac{F_{\text {vacuum }}}{F}=\frac{\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}, \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}}{\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon}, \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}}=\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}=\varepsilon_r{/tex}
where, {tex}\varepsilon_r{/tex} is called relative permittivity of the medium also called the dielectric constant of the medium.

In vector form,
{tex}\mathbf{\vec F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{|\mathbf{\vec r}|^2} \hat{\mathbf{r}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Coulomb's Law Basics Coulomb's law describes the force between two point charges. It states that the force varies inversely with the square of the distance between charges and is directly proportional to the product of their magnitudes.
text_html:
Coulomb's Law Basics
Coulomb's law describes the force between two point charges. It states that the force varies inversely with the square of the distance between charges and is directly proportional to the product of their magnitudes.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Point Charges Assumption When charged bodies have a much smaller linear size compared to their separation, they can be treated as point charges. This assumption simplifies computations by ignoring the body's size.
text_html:
Point Charges Assumption
When charged bodies have a much smaller linear size compared to their separation, they can be treated as point charges. This assumption simplifies computations by ignoring the body's size.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Mathematical Expression The magnitude of force (F) between two point charges q1 and q2 separated by distance r in vacuum is given by: {tex} F = k \frac{|q_1 q_2|}{r^2} {/tex} where k is a constant.
text_html:
Mathematical Expression
The magnitude of force (F) between two point charges q1 and q2 separated by distance r in vacuum is given by:
{tex} F = k \frac{|q_1 q_2|}{r^2} {/tex}
where k is a constant.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Torsion Balance Coulomb used a torsion balance to measure forces between charged metallic spheres. These measurements helped him draw conclusions about the nature of the forces acting between charges.
text_html:
Torsion Balance
Coulomb used a torsion balance to measure forces between charged metallic spheres. These measurements helped him draw conclusions about the nature of the forces acting between charges.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Charge Distribution Experiment A charged sphere placed in contact with an uncharged sphere evenly distributes the charge. For identical spheres, the final charge on each is half of the initial charged sphere's charge.
text_html:
Charge Distribution Experiment

  • A charged sphere placed in contact with an uncharged sphere evenly distributes the charge.
  • For identical spheres, the final charge on each is half of the initial charged sphere's charge.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: SI Units and Definition of Coulomb In SI units, k is approximately {tex}9 \times 10^9 \frac{{Nm}^2}{C^2}{/tex} . The unit of charge is called a coulomb (C). 1 C is the charge causing a force of {tex}9 \times 10^9{/tex} N at a 1 m distance in vacuum.
text_html:
SI Units and Definition of Coulomb
In SI units, k is approximately {tex}9 \times 10^9 \frac{{Nm}^2}{C^2}{/tex}.
The unit of charge is called a coulomb (C).
1 C is the charge causing a force of {tex}9 \times 10^9{/tex} N at a 1 m distance in vacuum.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Permittivity and Equation Adjustment For convenience, the constant k is expressed as: {tex} k = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} {/tex} where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} is the permittivity of free space, valued at {tex}8.854 \times 10^{-12} {C}^2 {N}^{-1} {m}^{-2}{/tex} .
text_html:
Permittivity and Equation Adjustment
For convenience, the constant k is expressed as:
{tex} k = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} {/tex}
where {tex}\varepsilon_0{/tex} is the permittivity of free space, valued at {tex}8.854 \times 10^{-12} {C}^2 {N}^{-1} {m}^{-2}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Vector Notation Position vectors of charges: r1 and r2. Forces between charges are related via vectors r21 and r12, with magnitudes r21 = r12. Unit vectors include {tex}\hat{r}_{21}{/tex} for direction.
text_html:
Vector Notation

  • Position vectors of charges: r1 and r2.
  • Forces between charges are related via vectors r21 and r12, with magnitudes r21 = r12.
  • Unit vectors include {tex}\hat{r}_{21}{/tex} for direction.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Equation in Vector Form Coulomb’s force law in vector notation is: {tex} {F}_{21}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{21}^2} \hat{r}_{21} {/tex} This law accounts for interactions without separate equations for like/unlike charges.
text_html:
Equation in Vector Form
Coulomb’s force law in vector notation is:
{tex} {F}_{21}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r_{21}^2} \hat{r}_{21} {/tex}
This law accounts for interactions without separate equations for like/unlike charges.

subject: Physics
topic: Coulomb's law-force between two point charges
text: Charge Interactions For same sign charges, the force is repulsive. For opposite signs, the force is attractive. Equation stands valid whether in vacuum or matter; matter influences come later.
text_html:
Charge Interactions

  • For same sign charges, the force is repulsive.
  • For opposite signs, the force is attractive.
  • Equation stands valid whether in vacuum or matter; matter influences come later.

subject: Physics
text: Coulomb's Law The mutual electric force between two charges is given by Coulomb's law, which describes the electrostatic interaction between charged objects.
text_html:
Coulomb's Law
The mutual electric force between two charges is given by Coulomb's law, which describes the electrostatic interaction between charged objects.

subject: Physics
text: Calculating Force with Multiple Charges To calculate the force on a charge when there are multiple surrounding charges, you must consider each force individually and sum them up as vectors.
text_html:
Calculating Force with Multiple Charges
To calculate the force on a charge when there are multiple surrounding charges, you must consider each force individually and sum them up as vectors.

subject: Physics
text: System of Charges When several stationary charges are present in vacuum, the principle of superposition is used to determine the force on a charge due to the others.
text_html:
System of Charges
When several stationary charges are present in vacuum, the principle of superposition is used to determine the force on a charge due to the others.

subject: Physics
text: Principle of Superposition The force on any charge due to a number of other charges is the vector sum of all the forces on that charge, considering one charge at a time.
text_html:
Principle of Superposition
The force on any charge due to a number of other charges is the vector sum of all the forces on that charge, considering one charge at a time.

subject: Physics
text: Force Calculation on a Charge In a three-charge system (q 1 , q 2 , q 3 ), the force on q 1 due to q 2 and q 3 is obtained by performing vector addition of individual forces.
text_html:
Force Calculation on a Charge
In a three-charge system (q1, q2, q3), the force on q1 due to q2 and q3 is obtained by performing vector addition of individual forces.

subject: Physics
text: Equation for Force Between Two Charges The force between charges q 1 and q 2 is given by: F 12 = (1/4πε₀) * (q 1 q 2 /r 12 2 ) * **r** 12
text_html:
Equation for Force Between Two Charges
The force between charges q1 and q2 is given by:

  • F12 = (1/4πε₀) * (q1q2/r122) * **r**12

subject: Physics
text: Total Force on a Charge The total force on q 1 due to q 2 and q 3 is: F 1 = F 12 + F 13 Equivalent to vector addition: F 1 = (1/4πε₀) * ((q 1 q 2 /r 12 2 ) * **r** 12 + (q 1 q 3 /r 13 2 ) * **r** 13 )
text_html:
Total Force on a Charge
The total force on q1 due to q2 and q3 is:

  • F1 = F12 + F13
    Equivalent to vector addition: F1 = (1/4πε₀) * ((q1q2/r122) * **r**12 + (q1q3/r132) * **r**13)

subject: Physics
text: Generalization to Multiple Charges The concept extends to more than three charges, as shown in more complex systems where the force is a sum of forces from each charge.
text_html:
Generalization to Multiple Charges
The concept extends to more than three charges, as shown in more complex systems where the force is a sum of forces from each charge.

subject: Physics
text: Equation for Total Force in a System The total force on q 1 due to all other charges is: F 1 = (q 1 /(4πε₀)) * Σ((q i /r 1i 2 ) * **r** 1i )
text_html:
Equation for Total Force in a System
The total force on q1 due to all other charges is:

  • F1 = (q1/(4πε₀)) * Σ((qi/r1i2) * **r**1i)

subject: Physics
text: Eletrostatics Foundation All of electrostatics fundamentally relies on Coulomb's law and the principle of superposition to explain forces between charges.
text_html:
Eletrostatics Foundation
All of electrostatics fundamentally relies on Coulomb's law and the principle of superposition to explain forces between charges.

subject: Physics
text: Electric field ( {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} ): The strength of an electric field is measured by the force experienced by a unit positive charge placed at that point. The direction of field is given by the direction of motion of a unit positive charge if it were free to move. {tex}\mathbf{\vec E}=\operatorname{Lim} \limits_{q_0 \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathbf{\vec F}}{q_0}{/tex} Here, q 0 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0, i.e. the test charge q 0 must be small, so that it does not produce its own electric field. SI unit of electric field intensity ( {tex}\vec E{/tex} ) is N/C and it is a vector quantity.
text_html:
Electric field ({tex}\vec{E}{/tex}): The strength of an electric field is measured by the force experienced by a unit positive charge placed at that point. The direction of field is given by the direction of motion of a unit positive charge if it were free to move.
{tex}\mathbf{\vec E}=\operatorname{Lim} \limits_{q_0 \rightarrow 0} \frac{\mathbf{\vec F}}{q_0}{/tex}
Here, q0 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 0, i.e. the test charge q0 must be small, so that it does not produce its own electric field.
SI unit of electric field intensity ({tex}\vec E{/tex}) is N/C and it is a vector quantity.

subject: Physics
text: Coulomb's Law and Point Charges Placing a point charge Q at the origin in a vacuum will exert force on another charge q placed at a point P at a distance r from charge Q.
text_html: Coulomb's Law and Point Charges Placing a point charge Q at the origin in a vacuum will exert force on another charge q placed at a point P at a distance r from charge Q.

subject: Physics
text: Concept of Electric Field To explain the force experienced by q in Q's presence, scientists proposed that Q creates an electric field, E, at each point in its surrounding.
text_html: Concept of Electric Field To explain the force experienced by q in Q's presence, scientists proposed that Q creates an electric field, E, at each point in its surrounding.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Field Equation The electric field E at a point r is given by: {tex} E(r) = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{r^2} \hat{r} {/tex} Here, \hat{r} is a vector pointing from the origin to the point r.
text_html: Electric Field Equation The electric field E at a point r is given by: {tex} E(r) = \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q}{r^2} \hat{r} {/tex} Here, \hat{r} is a vector pointing from the origin to the point r.

subject: Physics
text: Characteristics of Electric Field The electric field varies with position, indicating distributed quantity changes with space. The field has a spherical symmetry with charge Q at the center.
text_html: Characteristics of Electric Field The electric field varies with position, indicating distributed quantity changes with space. The field has a spherical symmetry with charge Q at the center.

subject: Physics
text: Force on a Charge A charge q experiences force F due to the electric field, given by: {tex} F(r) = qE(r) {/tex} The equation defines the SI unit of the electric field as N/C.
text_html: Force on a Charge A charge q experiences force F due to the electric field, given by: {tex} F(r) = qE(r) {/tex} The equation defines the SI unit of the electric field as N/C.

subject: Physics
text: Source and Test Charges A charge that creates an electric field is a source charge, while one that tests the field is a test charge, ideally very small to prevent moving the source.
text_html: Source and Test Charges A charge that creates an electric field is a source charge, while one that tests the field is a test charge, ideally very small to prevent moving the source.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Field Independence Electric field E due to source charge Q doesn't depend on test charge q, but on the position r.
text_html: Electric Field Independence Electric field E due to source charge Q doesn't depend on test charge q, but on the position r.

subject: Physics
text: Field Directions Electric fields from positive charges point outwards, while those from negative charges point inwards towards the charge.
text_html: Field Directions Electric fields from positive charges point outwards, while those from negative charges point inwards towards the charge.

subject: Physics
text: Field from Multiple Charges The field at a point due to multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields: {tex} E(r) = \sum \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_i}{r_{iP}^2} \hat{r}_{iP} {/tex}
text_html: Field from Multiple Charges The field at a point due to multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields: {tex} E(r) = \sum \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_i}{r_{iP}^2} \hat{r}_{iP} {/tex}

subject: Physics
text: Physical Significance of Field While not essential for electrostatics, electric fields are crucial in dynamic electromagnetic scenarios, accounting for time delays in interactions.
text_html: Physical Significance of Field While not essential for electrostatics, electric fields are crucial in dynamic electromagnetic scenarios, accounting for time delays in interactions.

subject: Physics
text: Electric field lines: It is the path straight or curved in electric field, such that tangent at any point of it gives direction of electric field at that point. Electric field lines are a way of pictorially mapping the electric field around a configuration of charge(s).
text_html: Electric field lines: It is the path straight or curved in electric field, such that tangent at any point of it gives direction of electric field at that point. Electric field lines are a way of pictorially mapping the electric field around a configuration of charge(s).

subject: Physics
text: Properties of electric field lines: Arbitrarily starts from +ve charge and end at -ve charge Continuous, but never form closed loops Tangent to elec. field line at any point gives direction of elec. field at that point. Never intersect because at the point of intersection, there will be two possible direction of elec. field which is not possible. Relative closeness of the field lines represents the magnitude of the field strength. Always normal to the surface of conductor.
text_html:
Properties of electric field lines:
Arbitrarily starts from +ve charge and end at -ve charge
Continuous, but never form closed loops
Tangent to elec. field line at any point gives direction of elec. field at that point.
Never intersect because at the point of intersection, there will be two possible direction of elec. field which is not possible.
Relative closeness of the field lines represents the magnitude of the field strength.
Always normal to the surface of conductor.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Field Representation Electric field is a vector quantity, represented by vectors. The field due to a point charge is demonstrated with vectors originating at the charge. These vectors decrease in length outwardly, reflecting field strength.
text_html: Electric Field Representation Electric field is a vector quantity, represented by vectors. The field due to a point charge is demonstrated with vectors originating at the charge. These vectors decrease in length outwardly, reflecting field strength.

subject: Physics
text: Field Lines and Direction Connect vectors in one direction to form a field line. These lines represent the electric field path, showing the force on a unit positive charge.
text_html: Field Lines and Direction Connect vectors in one direction to form a field line. These lines represent the electric field path, showing the force on a unit positive charge.

subject: Physics
text: Density of Field Lines Field lines density indicates the magnitude of the electric field. Closer lines show a stronger field near the charge; spread lines indicate a weaker field as the distance increases.
text_html: Density of Field Lines Field lines density indicates the magnitude of the electric field. Closer lines show a stronger field near the charge; spread lines indicate a weaker field as the distance increases.

subject: Physics
text: Field Lines in Three Dimensions Although represented in 2D, field lines exist in 3D. The density of field lines per unit cross-sectional area relates to field strength.
text_html: Field Lines in Three Dimensions Although represented in 2D, field lines exist in 3D. The density of field lines per unit cross-sectional area relates to field strength.

subject: Physics
text: Field Lines Consistency Field lines density remains consistent relating to strength. Thus, the number of lines crossing areas remains unchanged, pointing out the fundamental 1/r² relationship.
text_html: Field Lines Consistency Field lines density remains consistent relating to strength. Thus, the number of lines crossing areas remains unchanged, pointing out the fundamental 1/r² relationship.

subject: Physics
text: Field Lines and Solid Angles Solid angle subtends a small area, similar to a plane angle but in 3D. It defines how area relates to field lines, impacting the field's 1/r² dependence.
text_html: Field Lines and Solid Angles Solid angle subtends a small area, similar to a plane angle but in 3D. It defines how area relates to field lines, impacting the field's 1/r² dependence.

subject: Physics
text: Faraday's Field Lines Concept Field lines provide a method to visualize electric fields. Introduced by Faraday, these lines of force help in imagining electric fields around charge configurations.
text_html: Faraday's Field Lines Concept Field lines provide a method to visualize electric fields. Introduced by Faraday, these lines of force help in imagining electric fields around charge configurations.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Field Visualization Map electric fields with lines where a tangent at any point reflects the field direction. An arrow on the curve shows its direction, highlighting it as a space curve.
text_html: Electric Field Visualization Map electric fields with lines where a tangent at any point reflects the field direction. An arrow on the curve shows its direction, highlighting it as a space curve.

subject: Physics
text: Field Line Properties Field lines start at positive, end at negative charges. In charge-free spaces, they are continuous curves. Parallel fields never cross. Fields don’t form closed loops, supporting their conservative nature.
text_html:
Field Line Properties
  • Field lines start at positive, end at negative charges.
  • In charge-free spaces, they are continuous curves.
  • Parallel fields never cross.
  • Fields don’t form closed loops, supporting their conservative nature.

subject: Physics
text: Charge Configurations and Field Lines Field lines around charge arrangements vary: Single positive charges have outward fields, negative inward. Two positive charges exhibit repulsion. Opposing charges (a dipole) portray attraction.
text_html:
Charge Configurations and Field Lines Field lines around charge arrangements vary:
  • Single positive charges have outward fields, negative inward.
  • Two positive charges exhibit repulsion.
  • Opposing charges (a dipole) portray attraction.

subject: Physics
text: Work done in rotating the electric dipole from {tex}\theta_1{/tex} to {tex}\theta_2{/tex} is W = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex} ). Work done in rotating the dipole from the position of stable equilibrium to unstable equilibrium, i.e. when {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0 o and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex} . W = 2 pE Work done in rotating the dipole from the position of stable equilibrium to the position in which dipole experiences maximum torque, i.e. when {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0 o and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90 o . W = pE Electric flux. The electric flux through a small surface is defined as the electric lines of force passing through that are when held normally to the lines of force. Mathematically - {tex}\boldsymbol{\phi}=\vec{\boldsymbol{E}} \cdot \vec{\Delta \boldsymbol{S}}{/tex} Where E is the electric field and AS is the area vector representing the elementary surface area. Unit. In SI, unit of electric flux is (N m 2 C -2 ).
text_html:
Work done in rotating the electric dipole from {tex}\theta_1{/tex} to {tex}\theta_2{/tex} is W = pE (cos {tex}\theta_1{/tex} - cos {tex}\theta_2{/tex}). Work done in rotating the dipole from the position of stable equilibrium to unstable equilibrium, i.e. when {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0o and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = {tex}\pi{/tex}. W = 2 pE
Work done in rotating the dipole from the position of stable equilibrium to the position in which dipole experiences maximum torque, i.e. when {tex}\theta_1{/tex} = 0o and {tex}\theta_2{/tex} = 90o. W = pE
Electric flux. The electric flux through a small surface is defined as the electric lines of force passing through that are when held normally to the lines of force.
Mathematically - {tex}\boldsymbol{\phi}=\vec{\boldsymbol{E}} \cdot \vec{\Delta \boldsymbol{S}}{/tex}
Where E is the electric field and AS is the area vector representing the elementary surface area.
Unit. In SI, unit of electric flux is (N m2 C-2).

subject: Physics
text: Flow of Liquid Across a Surface The rate of flow of liquid across a flat surface is given by the expression {tex} v {~d} S {/tex} indicating the flux of liquid.
text_html:
Flow of Liquid Across a Surface
The rate of flow of liquid across a flat surface is given by the expression {tex} v {~d} S {/tex} indicating the flux of liquid.

subject: Physics
text: Flux with Inclined Normal When the surface normal is inclined at an angle {tex} \theta {/tex} to the flow, the effective area becomes {tex} \delta {d} S \cos \theta {/tex} .
text_html:
Flux with Inclined Normal
When the surface normal is inclined at an angle {tex} \theta {/tex} to the flow, the effective area becomes {tex} \delta {d} S \cos \theta {/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Defining Electric Flux The electric flux is analogous to liquid flux but represents lines' strength without any physical flow, given by {tex} E \Delta S \cos \theta {/tex} .
text_html:
Defining Electric Flux
The electric flux is analogous to liquid flux but represents lines' strength without any physical flow, given by {tex} E \Delta S \cos \theta {/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Field Lines and Area Element An area {tex} \Delta S {/tex} placed normal to field {tex} {E} {/tex} has a proportional number of field lines crossing it, reduced when tilted by angle {tex} \theta {/tex} ensuring {tex} E \Delta S \cos \theta {/tex} principles.
text_html:
Electric Field Lines and Area Element
An area {tex} \Delta S {/tex} placed normal to field {tex} {E} {/tex} has a proportional number of field lines crossing it, reduced when tilted by angle {tex} \theta {/tex} ensuring {tex} E \Delta S \cos \theta {/tex} principles.

subject: Physics
text: Importance of Area Orientation The flux depends on the orientation, similar to water flow through a ring, where orientation affects water volume passing through.
text_html:
Importance of Area Orientation
The flux depends on the orientation, similar to water flow through a ring, where orientation affects water volume passing through.

subject: Physics
text: Area Element as a Vector Area elements should be treated as vectors, considering magnitude and direction; the direction is along its normal.
text_html:
Area Element as a Vector
Area elements should be treated as vectors, considering magnitude and direction; the direction is along its normal.

subject: Physics
text: Normal Convention on Closed Surfaces For closed surfaces, the area vector direction is determined using the outward normal, simplifying direction choice.
text_html:
Normal Convention on Closed Surfaces
For closed surfaces, the area vector direction is determined using the outward normal, simplifying direction choice.

subject: Physics
text: Defining Electric Flux through Area Elements Electric flux {tex} \Delta \phi {/tex} through {tex} \Delta {S} {/tex} is defined by {tex} \Delta \phi={E} \cdot \Delta {S}=E \Delta S \cos \theta {/tex} , proportional to field lines crossing.
text_html:
Defining Electric Flux through Area Elements
Electric flux {tex} \Delta \phi {/tex} through {tex} \Delta {S} {/tex} is defined by {tex} \Delta \phi={E} \cdot \Delta {S}=E \Delta S \cos \theta {/tex}, proportional to field lines crossing.

subject: Physics
text: Total Electric Flux Calculation The total electric flux through surface {tex} S {/tex} is calculated using the sum {tex} \phi \simeq \Sigma {E} \cdot \Delta {S} {/tex} , transitioning to an integral as elements become infinitesimal.
text_html:
Total Electric Flux Calculation
The total electric flux through surface {tex} S {/tex} is calculated using the sum {tex} \phi \simeq \Sigma {E} \cdot \Delta {S} {/tex}, transitioning to an integral as elements become infinitesimal.

subject: Physics
text: Units of Electric Flux The unit of electric flux is {tex} {N} {C}^{-1} {~m}^2 {/tex} , derived from the fundamental definition in equation (1.11).
text_html:
Units of Electric Flux
The unit of electric flux is {tex} {N} {C}^{-1} {~m}^2 {/tex}, derived from the fundamental definition in equation (1.11).

subject: Physics
text: Electric Dipole: Two equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance.
text_html: Electric Dipole: Two equal and opposite charges separated by a small distance.

subject: Physics
text: Dipole moment: The strength of an electric dipole is measured by a vector quantity known as electric dipole moment (p) which is the product of the charge (q) and separation between the charges (2l). P = q {tex}\times{/tex} (2l) It is a vector whose direction is from negative to the positive charge
text_html:
Dipole moment: The strength of an electric dipole is measured by a vector quantity known as electric dipole moment (p) which is the product of the charge (q) and separation between the charges (2l).
P = q {tex}\times{/tex} (2l)
It is a vector whose direction is from negative to the positive charge

subject: Physics
text: Definition of an Electric Dipole An electric dipole consists of a pair of equal and opposite charges, +q and -q, separated by a distance of 2a. The direction of the dipole is conventionally from -q to +q.
text_html:
Definition of an Electric Dipole
An electric dipole consists of a pair of equal and opposite charges, +q and -q, separated by a distance of 2a. The direction of the dipole is conventionally from -q to +q.

subject: Physics
text: Centre of a Dipole The midpoint between charges +q and -q in an electric dipole is known as the centre of the dipole.
text_html:
Centre of a Dipole
The midpoint between charges +q and -q in an electric dipole is known as the centre of the dipole.

subject: Physics
text: Dipole Charge The total charge of an electric dipole is zero, although the individual fields of the charges do not cancel out at larger distances.
text_html:
Dipole Charge
The total charge of an electric dipole is zero, although the individual fields of the charges do not cancel out at larger distances.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Field of a Dipole The electric field of a dipole is expressed using Coulomb's law and follows superposition principles. It varies based on the point's position relative to the dipole axis and equatorial plane.
text_html:
Electric Field of a Dipole
The electric field of a dipole is expressed using Coulomb's law and follows superposition principles. It varies based on the point's position relative to the dipole axis and equatorial plane.

subject: Physics
text: Field on the Dipole Axis Positive charge field (E +q ): {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r-a)^2}{/tex} Negative charge field (E -q ): {tex}-\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r+a)^2}{/tex} Total field: {tex}\frac{4 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{r}{(r^2-a^2)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex}
text_html:
Field on the Dipole Axis

  • Positive charge field (E+q): {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r-a)^2}{/tex}
  • Negative charge field (E-q): {tex}-\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0(r+a)^2}{/tex}
  • Total field: {tex}\frac{4 q a}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{r}{(r^2-a^2)^2} \hat{{p}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
text: Field on the Equatorial Plane Magnitude of both fields: {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 (r^2+a^2)}{/tex} Total field along dipole axis opposite to {tex}\hat{{p}}{/tex} : {tex}-\frac{2qa}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 (r^2+a^2)^{3/2}} \hat{{p}}{/tex}
text_html:
Field on the Equatorial Plane

  • Magnitude of both fields: {tex}\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 (r^2+a^2)}{/tex}
  • Total field along dipole axis opposite to {tex}\hat{{p}}{/tex}: {tex}-\frac{2qa}{4\pi \varepsilon_0 (r^2+a^2)^{3/2}} \hat{{p}}{/tex}

subject: Physics
text: Dipole Moment Definition The dipole moment, p, is defined as q x 2a directed from -q to q. It is crucial in defining dipole field properties at long distances.
text_html:
Dipole Moment Definition
The dipole moment, p, is defined as q x 2a directed from -q to q. It is crucial in defining dipole field properties at long distances.

subject: Physics
text: Dipole's Field at Large Distances On Dipole Axis: {tex}\frac{2p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}{/tex} On Equatorial Plane: {tex}-\frac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}{/tex} At large distances, fields fall off with {tex}1/r^3{/tex} dependency.
text_html:
Dipole's Field at Large Distances

  • On Dipole Axis: {tex}\frac{2p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}{/tex}
  • On Equatorial Plane: {tex}-\frac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^3}{/tex}

At large distances, fields fall off with {tex}1/r^3{/tex} dependency.

subject: Physics
text: Point Dipoles A point dipole is defined as the limit where dipole size approaches zero, charge approaches infinity, but the dipole moment remains finite. This model applies universally at any distance.
text_html:
Point Dipoles
A point dipole is defined as the limit where dipole size approaches zero, charge approaches infinity, but the dipole moment remains finite. This model applies universally at any distance.

subject: Physics
text: Significance of Dipoles in Molecules Molecules with coinciding positive and negative charge centres have zero dipole moment, while those with separate centres, like water, have a permanent moment, affecting material properties.
text_html:
Significance of Dipoles in Molecules
Molecules with coinciding positive and negative charge centres have zero dipole moment, while those with separate centres, like water, have a permanent moment, affecting material properties.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Permanent Dipole in Uniform Field A permanent dipole has a dipole moment {tex} {p} {/tex} , which is independent of any external electric field {tex} {E} {/tex} .
text_html:
Permanent Dipole in Uniform Field
A permanent dipole has a dipole moment {tex} {p} {/tex}, which is independent of any external electric field {tex} {E} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Forces on the Dipole The dipole experiences a force {tex} q {E} {/tex} on {tex} q {/tex} and a force {tex} -q {E} {/tex} on {tex} -q {/tex} , but the net force is zero due to the uniformity of the field.
text_html:
Forces on the Dipole
The dipole experiences a force {tex} q {E} {/tex} on {tex} q {/tex} and a force {tex} -q {E} {/tex} on {tex} -q {/tex}, but the net force is zero due to the uniformity of the field.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Torque on the Dipole Due to separated charges, the forces create a torque that tends to align the dipole with the external field {tex} {E} {/tex} .
text_html:
Torque on the Dipole
Due to separated charges, the forces create a torque that tends to align the dipole with the external field {tex} {E} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Magnitude of Torque The torque is given by: {tex} \text {Magnitude of torque } = 2q a E \sin \theta {/tex} , where {tex} \theta {/tex} is the angle between {tex} {p} {/tex} and {tex} {E} {/tex} .
text_html:
Magnitude of Torque
The torque is given by: {tex} \text {Magnitude of torque } = 2q a E \sin \theta {/tex}, where {tex} \theta {/tex} is the angle between {tex} {p} {/tex} and {tex} {E} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Direction of Torque The direction of the torque is normal to the plane of the paper, coming out of it.
text_html:
Direction of Torque
The direction of the torque is normal to the plane of the paper, coming out of it.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Alignment of Dipole When {tex} {p} {/tex} is aligned with {tex} {E} {/tex} , the torque becomes zero.
text_html:
Alignment of Dipole
When {tex} {p} {/tex} is aligned with {tex} {E} {/tex}, the torque becomes zero.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Non-Uniform Field In a non-uniform field, the dipole experiences a net force and, generally, a torque, depending on its orientation with respect to {tex} {E} {/tex} .
text_html:
Non-Uniform Field
In a non-uniform field, the dipole experiences a net force and, generally, a torque, depending on its orientation with respect to {tex} {E} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Electric Force in Non-Uniform Field Figures show: {tex} {p} {/tex} parallel to {tex} {E} {/tex} : force towards increasing field. {tex} {p} {/tex} antiparallel to {tex} {E} {/tex} : force towards decreasing field.
text_html:
Electric Force in Non-Uniform Field
Figures show:

  • {tex} {p} {/tex} parallel to {tex} {E} {/tex}: force towards increasing field.
  • {tex} {p} {/tex} antiparallel to {tex} {E} {/tex}: force towards decreasing field.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Frictional Electricity A comb attracts paper when run through hair due to the charged comb polarizing the paper, thereby inducing a dipole moment.
text_html:
Frictional Electricity
A comb attracts paper when run through hair due to the charged comb polarizing the paper, thereby inducing a dipole moment.

subject: Physics
topic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
text: Induced Dipole and Movement The induced dipole in the paper moves towards the comb due to the non-uniform electric field created by the comb.
text_html:
Induced Dipole and Movement
The induced dipole in the paper moves towards the comb due to the non-uniform electric field created by the comb.

subject: Physics
text: Discrete vs Continuous Charge Distributions The problem set initially focuses on discrete charges which are simpler mathematically, but continuous charge distributions are necessary in some practical scenarios.
text_html:
Discrete vs Continuous Charge Distributions
The problem set initially focuses on discrete charges which are simpler mathematically, but continuous charge distributions are necessary in some practical scenarios.

subject: Physics
text: Surface Charge Distribution In a surface charge distribution, consider a small area element {tex}\Delta S{/tex} , where charge {tex}\Delta Q{/tex} is assigned to calculate surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} as {tex}\sigma=\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta S}{/tex} .
text_html:
Surface Charge Distribution
In a surface charge distribution, consider a small area element {tex}\Delta S{/tex}, where charge {tex}\Delta Q{/tex} is assigned to calculate surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} as {tex}\sigma=\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta S}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Surface Charge Density The surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} is a macroscopic quantity that averages out the microscopic charge density over the area element, with units in {tex}\frac{C}{m^2}{/tex} .
text_html:
Surface Charge Density
The surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} is a macroscopic quantity that averages out the microscopic charge density over the area element, with units in {tex}\frac{C}{m^2}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Line and Volume Charge Densities The linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} is defined for wires as {tex}\lambda=\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta l}{/tex} , with units in {tex}\frac{C}{m}{/tex} . Volume charge density {tex}\rho{/tex} is defined as {tex}\rho=\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta V}{/tex} , with units in {tex}\frac{C}{m^3}{/tex} .
text_html:
Line and Volume Charge Densities

  • The linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} is defined for wires as {tex}\lambda=\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta l}{/tex}, with units in {tex}\frac{C}{m}{/tex}.
  • Volume charge density {tex}\rho{/tex} is defined as {tex}\rho=\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta V}{/tex}, with units in {tex}\frac{C}{m^3}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Continuous Charge Distribution This concept is similar to continuous mass distribution. Charge density {tex}\rho{/tex} varies over different points, considered over macroscopically small volume elements containing numerous microscopic charges.
text_html:
Continuous Charge Distribution
This concept is similar to continuous mass distribution. Charge density {tex}\rho{/tex} varies over different points, considered over macroscopically small volume elements containing numerous microscopic charges.

subject: Physics
text: Field Calculation for Continuous Distributions To find the electric field stemming from continuous charge distributions, the problem utilizes Coulomb's law and superposition principle.
text_html:
Field Calculation for Continuous Distributions
To find the electric field stemming from continuous charge distributions, the problem utilizes Coulomb's law and superposition principle.

subject: Physics
text: Coulomb's Law Application Electric field due to a charge element {tex}\rho \Delta V{/tex} is determined using Coulomb's law as {tex}\Delta {E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\rho \Delta V}{r^{\prime 2}} \hat{{r}}^{\prime}{/tex} .
text_html:
Coulomb's Law Application
Electric field due to a charge element {tex}\rho \Delta V{/tex} is determined using Coulomb's law as
{tex}\Delta {E}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\rho \Delta V}{r^{\prime 2}} \hat{{r}}^{\prime}{/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Superposition Principle The resultant electric field from a charge distribution is obtained by summing the electric fields due to individual volume elements {tex} {E} \cong \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \sum\limits_{\text {all } \Delta V} \frac{\rho \Delta V}{r^{\prime 2}} \hat{{r}}^{\prime} {/tex} .
text_html:
Superposition Principle
The resultant electric field from a charge distribution is obtained by summing the electric fields due to individual volume elements
{tex} {E} \cong \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \sum\limits_{\text {all } \Delta V} \frac{\rho \Delta V}{r^{\prime 2}} \hat{{r}}^{\prime} {/tex}.

subject: Physics
text: Mathematical Considerations In a rigorous method, small volume elements can entail taking {tex}\Delta V \rightarrow 0{/tex} , but sometimes this step is simplified for easier understanding.
text_html:
Mathematical Considerations
In a rigorous method, small volume elements can entail taking {tex}\Delta V \rightarrow 0{/tex}, but sometimes this step is simplified for easier understanding.

subject: Physics
text: Applications of Field from Charge Distributions The methodology for calculating electric fields can be applied to entirely discrete, entirely continuous, or mixed charge distributions.
text_html:
Applications of Field from Charge Distributions

  • The methodology for calculating electric fields can be applied to entirely discrete, entirely continuous, or mixed charge distributions.

subject: Physics
text: Gauss' theorem: It states that the total outward electric flux through a closed surface is {tex}\frac {1}{\in_0}{/tex} times the charge enclosed by the closed surface. Mathematically: {tex}\oint \vec{\boldsymbol{E}} \cdot \vec{\boldsymbol{dS}}=\frac{q}{\epsilon_o}{/tex} where q is the charge enclosed by the closed surface.
text_html:
Gauss' theorem: It states that the total outward electric flux through a closed surface is {tex}\frac {1}{\in_0}{/tex} times the charge enclosed by the closed surface.
Mathematically: {tex}\oint \vec{\boldsymbol{E}} \cdot \vec{\boldsymbol{dS}}=\frac{q}{\epsilon_o}{/tex} where q is the charge enclosed by the closed surface.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Flux through a Sphere The total electric flux through a sphere enclosing a point charge at its center is calculated using Coulomb's Law for the electric field:
text_html:
Electric Flux through a Sphere
The total electric flux through a sphere enclosing a point charge at its center is calculated using Coulomb's Law for the electric field:

subject: Physics
text: Area Element and Flux The flux through an area element ΔS of a sphere, given by: ΔΦ = E • ΔS = (q / 4πε₀r²) ΔS
text_html:
Area Element and Flux
The flux through an area element ΔS of a sphere, given by:

  • ΔΦ = E • ΔS = (q / 4πε₀r²) ΔS

subject: Physics
text: Total Flux through Sphere The total electric flux ∅ through the sphere is: ∅ = (q / ε₀)
text_html:
Total Flux through Sphere
The total electric flux ∅ through the sphere is:
∅ = (q / ε₀)

subject: Physics
text: Gauss's Law States that the total electric flux through a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed divided by ε₀.
text_html:
Gauss's Law
States that the total electric flux through a closed surface is equal to the charge enclosed divided by ε₀.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Flux for Uniform Field Φ = Φ₁ + Φ₂ + Φ₃ Φ₁ and Φ₂ are flux through circular surfaces with axes parallel to field E. Φ₃ through the curved surface is zero as its normal is perpendicular to E.
text_html:
Electric Flux for Uniform Field

  • Φ = Φ₁ + Φ₂ + Φ₃
  • Φ₁ and Φ₂ are flux through circular surfaces with axes parallel to field E.
  • Φ₃ through the curved surface is zero as its normal is perpendicular to E.

subject: Physics
text: Gauss's Law Implications When the electric flux through a closed surface is zero, it indicates no net charge within the surface.
text_html:
Gauss's Law Implications
When the electric flux through a closed surface is zero, it indicates no net charge within the surface.

subject: Physics
text: Important Aspects of Gauss's Law 1. It's valid for any closed surface. 2. Encloses all charges within the surface and considers external influences. 3. The Gaussian surface should not intersect discrete charges.
text_html:
Important Aspects of Gauss's Law
1. It's valid for any closed surface.
2. Encloses all charges within the surface and considers external influences.
3. The Gaussian surface should not intersect discrete charges.

subject: Physics
text: Gaussian Surface Defined as the imagined surface used to simplify the calculation of electric fields. It must not pass through discrete charges, although continuous charge distributions are allowed.
text_html:
Gaussian Surface
Defined as the imagined surface used to simplify the calculation of electric fields. It must not pass through discrete charges, although continuous charge distributions are allowed.

subject: Physics
text: Symmetry in Gauss's Law Utilizing symmetry and Gaussian surfaces simplifies complex electrostatic field calculations.
text_html:
Symmetry in Gauss's Law
Utilizing symmetry and Gaussian surfaces simplifies complex electrostatic field calculations.

subject: Physics
text: Foundation of Gauss's Law Relies on the inverse square relation of Coulomb's Law; deviations in results hint at discrepancies in the law.
text_html:
Foundation of Gauss's Law
Relies on the inverse square relation of Coulomb's Law; deviations in results hint at discrepancies in the law.

subject: Physics
text: Electric field due to infinitely long straight linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex} E = {tex}\frac {\lambda} {2 \pi \epsilon_o r}{/tex} where r is perpendicular distance of the observation point from the wire.
text_html:
Electric field due to infinitely long straight linear charge density {tex}\lambda{/tex}
E = {tex}\frac {\lambda} {2 \pi \epsilon_o r}{/tex}
where r is perpendicular distance of the observation point from the wire.

subject: Physics
text: Electric field due to an infinite plane sheet of charge of surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} Electric field between two infinite plane parallel sheets of charge of surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-\sigma{/tex} : E= {tex}\frac {\sigma}{2 \epsilon_o}{/tex}
text_html:
Electric field due to an infinite plane sheet of charge of surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex}
Electric field between two infinite plane parallel sheets of charge of surface charge density {tex}\sigma{/tex} and {tex}-\sigma{/tex}:
E= {tex}\frac {\sigma}{2 \epsilon_o}{/tex}

subject: Physics
text: Electric Field Due to Charge Distribution The electric field from general charge distribution can't always be calculated for every space point. Symmetric configurations can utilize Gauss's law for simpler calculations.
text_html: Electric Field Due to Charge Distribution The electric field from general charge distribution can't always be calculated for every space point. Symmetric configurations can utilize Gauss's law for simpler calculations.

subject: Physics
text: Infinite Straight Uniformly Charged Wire An infinitely long straight wire with uniform linear charge density creates a radial electric field. ***E's*** direction is radial, outward if ***λ > 0***, inward if ***λ < 0***.
text_html: Infinite Straight Uniformly Charged Wire An infinitely long straight wire with uniform linear charge density creates a radial electric field. ***E's*** direction is radial, outward if ***λ > 0***, inward if ***λ < 0***.

subject: Physics
text: Radial Nature of Electric Field in Wire Electric fields from wire elements create a resultant radial electric field, unaffected by point locations along the wire length.
text_html: Radial Nature of Electric Field in Wire Electric fields from wire elements create a resultant radial electric field, unaffected by point locations along the wire length.

subject: Physics
text: Using Gaussian Surface For a thin wire, a cylindrical Gaussian surface helps calculate the electric field ***E = λ / (2π ε₀ r) ***.
text_html: Using Gaussian Surface For a thin wire, a cylindrical Gaussian surface helps calculate the electric field ***E = λ / (2π ε₀ r) ***.

subject: Physics
text: Electric Field with Infinite Plane Sheet A uniformly charged infinite plane sheet has a perpendicular electric field. ***E = σ / (2ε₀)*** and is indifferent to ***y***, ***z*** coordinates.
text_html: Electric Field with Infinite Plane Sheet A uniformly charged infinite plane sheet has a perpendicular electric field. ***E = σ / (2ε₀)*** and is indifferent to ***y***, ***z*** coordinates.

subject: Physics
text: Symmetric Field Calculation For a plane, a rectangular or cylindrical Gaussian surface ensures only two faces contribute to flux, yielding simplified calculations.
text_html: Symmetric Field Calculation For a plane, a rectangular or cylindrical Gaussian surface ensures only two faces contribute to flux, yielding simplified calculations.

subject: Physics
text: Charged Spherical Shell: Outside Field With radius ***R***, the field outside the shell is ***E = q / (4π ε₀ r²)*** same as placing charge at the center, depending on ***q***'s sign.
text_html: Charged Spherical Shell: Outside Field With radius ***R***, the field outside the shell is ***E = q / (4π ε₀ r²)*** same as placing charge at the center, depending on ***q***'s sign.

subject: Physics
text: Inside Field of Spherical Shell Inside, the electric field ***E = 0*** due to the Gaussian surface encasing no charge, supporting Coulomb's law.
text_html: Inside Field of Spherical Shell Inside, the electric field ***E = 0*** due to the Gaussian surface encasing no charge, supporting Coulomb's law.

subject: Physics
text: Equivalence with Center Charge Field outside a spherical shell behaves as if the entire charge is concentrated at its center, confirmable using Gauss's law.
text_html: Equivalence with Center Charge Field outside a spherical shell behaves as if the entire charge is concentrated at its center, confirmable using Gauss's law.

subject: Physics
text: Gauss's Law Verification of Coulomb's Law Inside a shell's uniform charge distribution, zero field confirms the ***1/r²*** dependence in Coulomb's law.
text_html: Gauss's Law Verification of Coulomb's Law Inside a shell's uniform charge distribution, zero field confirms the ***1/r²*** dependence in Coulomb's law.

subject: Physics
text: Sparks and Crackles Experience sparks or crackles when removing synthetic clothing or sweaters. Most noticeable in dry weather conditions.
text_html:
Sparks and Crackles
  • Experience sparks or crackles when removing synthetic clothing or sweaters.
  • Most noticeable in dry weather conditions.

subject: Physics
text: Lightning and Electric Discharges Lightning in the sky is an example of an electric discharge. Electric discharges can also occur through our body. Occurs often when touching metal objects after movement.
text_html:
Lightning and Electric Discharges
  • Lightning in the sky is an example of an electric discharge.
  • Electric discharges can also occur through our body.
  • Occurs often when touching metal objects after movement.

subject: Physics
text: Static Electricity and Its Effects Electric shock sensations from touching surfaces after sliding. Generated by the rubbing of insulating surfaces. Results in the accumulation of electric charges.
text_html:
Static Electricity and Its Effects
  • Electric shock sensations from touching surfaces after sliding.
  • Generated by the rubbing of insulating surfaces.
  • Results in the accumulation of electric charges.

subject: Physics
text: Understanding Static Static electricity arises from charges that do not move with time. Electrostatics is the study of these static charges.
text_html:
Understanding Static
  • Static electricity arises from charges that do not move with time.
  • Electrostatics is the study of these static charges.

subject: Physics
text: Core Concepts of Electrostatics Deals with forces and fields generated by stationary charges. Explores potentials arising due to these static charges.
text_html:
Core Concepts of Electrostatics
  • Deals with forces and fields generated by stationary charges.
  • Explores potentials arising due to these static charges.
